math.title: Mathematics
math.subtitle: Week 2: Metric Area and Geometry Basics

slides.title: Standard Units of Area
slides.text: Let's learn about standard metric units for area. Instead of using arbitrary tiles, we use standard square centimeters and square meters to measure flat surfaces accurately. In math, we express this as: Area units = standard measurements. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why do we need standard units instead of using any size of tile?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Area units = standard measurements.

slides.title: The Square Centimeter
slides.text: A square centimeter is a metric unit of area. It represents the space covered by a tiny square where each of the four sides is exactly one centimeter long. In math, we express this as: 1 sq. cm = 1 cm x 1 cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you name three small objects you can measure in square centimeters?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: 1 sq. cm = 1 cm x 1 cm.

slides.title: Measuring in Square Centimeters
slides.text: We use square centimeters to measure small surfaces like books, index cards, or smartphones. We multiply the length and width in centimeters to find the area. In math, we express this as: Area = 6 cm x 4 cm = 24 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of a photo that is 10 cm long and 8 cm wide?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = 6 cm x 4 cm = 24 sq. cm.

slides.title: The Square Meter
slides.text: A square meter is a larger metric unit of area. It is the space covered by a large square where each of the four sides is exactly one meter long. In math, we express this as: 1 sq. m = 1 m x 1 m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Would you measure the floor of your classroom in centimeters or meters?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: 1 sq. m = 1 m x 1 m.

slides.title: Measuring in Square Meters
slides.text: We use square meters to measure larger surfaces like room floors, gardens, or school blackboards. We multiply the length and width in meters to find the area. In math, we express this as: Area = 5 m x 4 m = 20 sq. m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of a rectangular garden that is 6 m long and 4 m wide?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = 5 m x 4 m = 20 sq. m.

slides.title: Estimating Metric Area
slides.text: We can estimate metric areas by comparing them to familiar objects. For example, a student desk is about one-half of a square meter in surface area. In math, we express this as: Estimate = close guess of area in metric units. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How can you estimate the floor area of your bedroom without a ruler?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: Estimate = close guess of area in metric units.

slides.title: Comparing Area Units
slides.text: It is important to choose the right unit. Measuring a large room in square centimeters would give a huge number, while a notebook in square meters is too small. In math, we express this as: 1 sq. m = 10,000 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why is it better to measure a school playground in square meters?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: 1 sq. m = 10,000 sq. cm.

slides.title: Drawing a Metric Square
slides.text: Let's draw a square with a side of 3 centimeters using a ruler. We draw four equal sides of 3 cm, meeting at perfect square corners. In math, we express this as: Area = 3 cm x 3 cm = 9 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of the square you just drew on your paper?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = 3 cm x 3 cm = 9 sq. cm.

slides.title: Drawing a Metric Rectangle
slides.text: Let's draw a rectangle with a length of 5 centimeters and a width of 3 centimeters. We draw opposite sides equal and straight using our ruler. In math, we express this as: Area = 5 cm x 3 cm = 15 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you calculate the area of the drawn rectangle in square centimeters?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = 5 cm x 3 cm = 15 sq. cm.

slides.title: Introducing Geometry: The Point
slides.text: In geometry, a point represents an exact position or location. We draw it as a small dot and name it using a single capital letter, like Point A. In math, we express this as: Point = exact location in space. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you find a point on a map showing where your school is?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Point = exact location in space.

slides.title: Points in the Real World
slides.text: We see models of points everywhere. A tiny pin on a digital map, a dot on a paper, or a star in the night sky all represent geometric points. In math, we express this as: Dot = Point location. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How does a point help us mark a precise location on a grid?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Dot = Point location.

slides.title: Introducing the Line
slides.text: A geometric line is a straight path of points that extends forever in opposite directions. To show it has no end, we draw arrowheads on both ends. In math, we express this as: Line = straight path that goes on forever. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How do the two arrowheads show that a line never stops going?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Line = straight path that goes on forever.

slides.title: Representing Lines
slides.text: We name a line by picking two points on it, like A and B, and writing a small line symbol with two arrows above the letters, like Line AB. In math, we express this as: Line AB = straight path through A and B. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you draw a straight line and label two points on it?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: Line AB = straight path through A and B.

slides.title: The Line Segment
slides.text: A line segment is a part of a line that has two fixed endpoints. It has a definite length, which means we can measure it using a metric ruler. In math, we express this as: Segment AB = path between endpoints A and B. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why can we measure a line segment but not a whole line?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: Segment AB = path between endpoints A and B.

slides.title: Measuring Line Segments
slides.text: We measure a line segment by placing the zero mark of our ruler at one endpoint and reading the number at the other endpoint of the segment. In math, we express this as: Length = 8 cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. A straight line goes on forever in opposite directions, which we show with two arrowheads. What is the length of a pencil segment when you measure it?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: Length = 8 cm.

slides.title: Introducing the Ray
slides.text: A ray is a part of a line that starts at one endpoint and extends forever in only one direction. We show this with one arrowhead at the end. In math, we express this as: Ray AB = starts at A, goes forever through B. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How does a ray differ from a line and a line segment?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: Ray AB = starts at A, goes forever through B.

slides.title: Rays in Daily Life
slides.text: We see models of rays in our daily environment. A beam of light coming from a flashlight or sunlight shining down on Mount Matutum represents a ray. In math, we express this as: Sunbeam = model of a geometric ray. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why does a flashlight beam act like a ray in geometry?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: Sunbeam = model of a geometric ray.

slides.title: Comparing Segments and Rays
slides.text: Let's compare: a line segment has two endpoints and can be measured. A ray has only one endpoint and goes on forever, so it cannot be measured. In math, we express this as: Segment = 2 endpoints, Ray = 1 endpoint. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Which of these two has a definite length that you can write down?

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: Segment = 2 endpoints, Ray = 1 endpoint.

slides.title: T'nalak Weaving Patterns
slides.text: Weavers in Lake Sebu create beautiful geometric patterns. They use straight lines and points in their designs to make beautiful traditional T'nalak cloths. In math, we express this as: Geometric designs = points and segments. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. A straight line goes on forever in opposite directions, which we show with two arrowheads. Can you spot straight line segments in traditional hand-woven mats?

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: Geometric designs = points and segments.

slides.title: City Layout Maps
slides.text: City planners in Koronadal City use points and lines to design maps. Points show buildings, and lines show streets that connect different places in the town. In math, we express this as: Street layout = lines and intersections. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How do points and lines make maps easier for people to read?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Street layout = lines and intersections.

slides.title: Fish Cage Grids in Lake Sebu
slides.text: Fishermen in Lake Sebu layout rectangular fish cages in the lake water. They measure the area in square meters to decide how many tilapia fish to raise. In math, we express this as: Area = 10 m x 10 m = 100 sq. m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. If a fish cage is 10 meters on each side, what is area?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = 10 m x 10 m = 100 sq. m.

slides.title: Drawing Parallel Paths
slides.text: We can draw two straight paths that stay the same distance apart using our ruler. These paths show us how lines can run side-by-side without crossing. In math, we express this as: Distance = constant between straight paths. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you draw two straight paths that never meet on your paper?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Distance = constant between straight paths.

slides.title: Geometry Tools in Action
slides.text: Using the right tools is important in geometry. A sharp pencil and a flat ruler help us draw clean points, straight lines, and precise line segments. In math, we express this as: Ruler + Pencil = precise geometric drawings. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. A straight line goes on forever in opposite directions, which we show with two arrowheads. Why is a flat ruler necessary when drawing geometric figures?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Ruler + Pencil = precise geometric drawings.

slides.title: Solving Metric Area Problems
slides.text: Let's solve an area problem. A rectangular classroom floor is 8 meters long and 5 meters wide. We multiply 8 by 5 to find the floor area. In math, we express this as: Area = 8 m x 5 m = 40 sq. m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of a notebook that is 15 cm by 10 cm?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = 8 m x 5 m = 40 sq. m.

slides.title: Summary of Week 2 Lessons
slides.text: Let's review our lessons. We learned about standard area units (sq. cm and sq. m), drawing shapes, and recognizing basic geometric elements: points, lines, segments, and rays. In math, we express this as: Area = metric space, Geometry = points and lines. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How do geometry and area help us describe the physical world?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Area = metric space, Geometry = points and lines.

Exercise 1: Metric Area Problems

Calculate the area using the formula and write your answer with the correct unit (sq. cm or sq. m).

1. A rectangular tuna packing box in GenSan is 80 cm long and 50 cm wide. What is its area?
4000 sq. cm

Exercise 2: Identifying Geometry Figures

Look at the drawings and write whether it is a Point, Line, Line Segment, or Ray.

1. A straight path with arrowheads on both ends:
Line
2. A straight path with two solid endpoints:
Line Segment

Exercise 3: Geometry Applications

Write the name of the real-world model represented by each geometric concept.

1. A beam of light coming from a flashlight:
Ray

performance.title: Geometry and Area Map of a Park
performance.desc: Design a small park layout in Koronadal City on paper. Draw a rectangular playground that is 10 cm long and 8 cm wide, and calculate its area. Near the playground, draw a straight walking path (representing a line segment), a streetlamp shining light downwards (representing a ray), and a fountain marker (representing a point). Label all figures clearly, take a photo, and upload it.
labels: Playground area calculation (80 sq. cm)
Correct labels for Point, Segment, and Ray
Clean drawing design

science.title: Science
science.subtitle: Week 2: Asking Questions Like a Scientist

slides.title: Welcome to Inquiry!
slides.text: Hello, Explorer! Welcome to Science Week 2! This week we learn to ask questions like real scientists. Scientists are curious about everything they see in nature. They start their investigations by asking why and how. We will learn how to tinker with common materials. Tinkering means touching and testing things to see changes. Let's prepare our curiosity and get ready to ask. Are you ready to explore and learn with us today? For instance, consider the example of Goal: Formulate scientific questions by exploring and tinkering with materials. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Goal
examples.content: Formulate scientific questions by exploring and tinkering with materials.

slides.title: Curiosity and Inquiry
slides.text: Inquiry is the heart of all scientific discoveries. It begins when you observe something and ask questions. Why does the ice cube melt on the wooden desk? How does a heavy metal key sink in the water? Curiosity drives us to search for answers in nature. We ask questions that we can test through experiments. Avoid asking opinions, like 'Is this flower beautiful?' Let's practice forming questions about what we see today! For instance, consider the example of Inquiry: Asking: 'Why does wet sand feel colder than dry sand?' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Inquiry
examples.content: Asking: 'Why does wet sand feel colder than dry sand?'

slides.title: Tinkering with Materials
slides.text: Tinkering is a hands-on way to explore physical things. We play with materials to observe how they behave. We press clay, stretch bands, and drop heavy keys. This action helps us see what properties materials hold. Squeezing a dry sponge shows it has air holes. Squeezing a wet sponge shows it holds liquid inside. Tinkering helps us form solid questions for tests. Tinker with three objects in your room safely today! For instance, consider the example of Tinkering: Pressing modeling clay with a spoon to leave hollow shapes. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Tinkering
examples.content: Pressing modeling clay with a spoon to leave hollow shapes.

slides.title: Tinkering with Modeling Clay
slides.text: Modeling clay is a solid material we can shape. Tinkering with clay shows it changes shape easily. We press it, roll it, and pinch it with fingers. The clay holds the new shape we give to it. Why does clay stay bent while rubber bands snap back? This is a great scientific question to investigate. We apply force using our hands to shape the clay. Observe how your stylus can press clay to write names! For instance, consider the example of Clay Test: Pressing a fork into clay leaves a lined print on its surface. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Clay Test
examples.content: Pressing a fork into clay leaves a lined print on its surface.

slides.title: Tinkering with Balloons
slides.text: Balloons are elastic materials that hold invisible air. Tinkering with balloons shows air occupies physical space. Blowing air inside makes the balloon stretch and expand. The air pushes outwards against the balloon walls. This pushing force is called air pressure in science. Squeezing the inflated balloon changes its outer shape. Letting go of the neck makes the balloon fly away. Why does releasing the air make the balloon move? For instance, consider the example of Balloon Test: Air escaping from the neck pushes the balloon forward. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Balloon Test
examples.content: Air escaping from the neck pushes the balloon forward.

slides.title: Tinkering with Sponges
slides.text: A dry sponge is light and full of tiny holes. These holes are filled with invisible air molecules. Dipping the sponge in water shows absorption properties. The sponge sucks in liquid, displacing the air inside. You will observe tiny air bubbles rising in water. Squeezing the wet sponge releases the absorbed water. A wet sponge feels much heavier than a dry one. Why does water occupy the empty holes of sponges? For instance, consider the example of Sponge Test: Squeezing a wet sponge returns it to its light, dry state. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Sponge Test
examples.content: Squeezing a wet sponge returns it to its light, dry state.

slides.title: Tinkering with Magnets
slides.text: Magnets hold an invisible force called magnetism. Tinkering with magnets shows they pull certain metals. They pull iron nails, paperclips, and steel keys. They do not pull wooden sticks or plastic toys. This pulling force is called magnetic attraction in science. Magnets can pull objects even through thin paper. Why do magnets only attract iron and steel metals? Test different objects in your room with magnets safely! For instance, consider the example of Magnet Test: A magnet pulls steel paperclips across the desk surface. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Magnet Test
examples.content: A magnet pulls steel paperclips across the desk surface.

slides.title: Tinkering with Paper Planes
slides.text: Folding paper into planes shows aerodynamics in action. Tinkering with shapes changes how far planes fly. Thin, pointed planes cut through air very fast. Wide, flat planes float slowly but land soon. Air pushes against the paper wings to lift planes. This upward pushing force is called air resistance. Why does a pointed nose make planes fly further? Fold two different plane shapes and test them now! For instance, consider the example of Plane Test: Comparing a pointed dart plane with a wide glider plane. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Plane Test
examples.content: Comparing a pointed dart plane with a wide glider plane.

slides.title: Tinkering with Floating Objects
slides.text: Tinkering with water shows objects float or sink. Buoyancy determines if things stay on top or go down. Dry leaves, twigs, and plastic spoons float on water. Iron keys, stones, and glass marbles sink to bottoms. Why do heavy metal keys sink while large logs float? This question leads to density concepts in science. Test a coin and a plastic bottle cap in buckets. Observe which one floats and which one sinks down! For instance, consider the example of Float Test: A plastic bottle cap floats because it is light and holds air. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Float Test
examples.content: A plastic bottle cap floats because it is light and holds air.

slides.title: Tinkering with Dissolving Solids
slides.text: Mixing solids in water shows how they dissolve. Tinkering with sugar reveals it mixes completely. Sugar crystals become invisible when stirred in water. This mixtures is called a solution in daily science. Does sugar dissolve faster in hot or cold water? Stirring makes sugar dissolve much quicker than sitting. Why does heat speed up the dissolving process? Observe how salt behaves when stirred in water jars! For instance, consider the example of Dissolving Test: Stirring sugar in warm water makes it dissolve in seconds. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Dissolving Test
examples.content: Stirring sugar in warm water makes it dissolve in seconds.

slides.title: Tinkering with Flashlights
slides.text: Tinkering with light sources changes shadow sizes. Shine a flashlight at a toy figurine on tables. Observe the dark shadow formed on the wall behind. Moving the light closer makes the shadow grow larger. Moving the light further away makes the shadow smaller. Tilting the flashlight changes the shadow's direction. Why does moving the light change the shadow's scale? Draw the different shadow sizes on your paper page! For instance, consider the example of Light Test: Closest light source creates the largest and dimmest shadow. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Light Test
examples.content: Closest light source creates the largest and dimmest shadow.

slides.title: Tinkering with Spinning Tops
slides.text: Spinning tops show rotational motion and friction. We spin a plastic top on a smooth table. Circular force keeps the top standing upright while spinning. The tip of the top rubs against table surfaces. This rubbing force is called friction in daily science. Friction slows down the top until it falls over. Why does a smooth table make tops spin longer? Compare spinning on rough carpets and smooth tables! For instance, consider the example of Spin Test: Rough surfaces create high friction, stopping tops quickly. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spin Test
examples.content: Rough surfaces create high friction, stopping tops quickly.

slides.title: Tinkering with Mirrors
slides.text: Mirrors reflect light to show clear images. A mirror has a flat, extremely smooth surface. Light bounces off this surface back to your eyes. Tilting the mirror changes what objects you observe. Why do we see our reflection in glass mirrors? Rough wood does not reflect light to show images. Rough surfaces scatter light waves in all directions. Explore reflections using shiny metal spoons in kitchens! For instance, consider the example of Mirror Test: Bouncing light off smooth surfaces creates reflection images. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Mirror Test
examples.content: Bouncing light off smooth surfaces creates reflection images.

slides.title: Tinkering with Elastic Bands
slides.text: Rubber bands stretch when we apply pulling forces. This property of materials is called elasticity. The band snaps back to its shape when released. Plucking a stretched band creates rapid vibrations. These vibrations produce a hum sound in the air. Stretching the band tighter makes a higher pitch sound. Why does tension change the pitch of sound waves? Hold one end with teeth and pluck bands safely! For instance, consider the example of Band Test: Vibrating rubber bands create sound waves we hear easily. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Band Test
examples.content: Vibrating rubber bands create sound waves we hear easily.

slides.title: Tinkering with Bent Stems
slides.text: Plants respond to light sources in their environment. Tinker with a small potted plant near windows. Observe that stems bend towards the bright sunlight. Turn the pot around so stems face rooms. In a few days, stems bend back to windows. Why do plant stems grow towards the light source? Plants need sunlight to survive and make food. This growth response is called phototropism in science! For instance, consider the example of Stem Test: Stems bend towards sunlight to maximize light absorption. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Stem Test
examples.content: Stems bend towards sunlight to maximize light absorption.

slides.title: Tinkering with Melting Ice
slides.text: Ice cubes melt into liquid water at room temperature. Tinker with ice cubes placed in different spots. Put one cube in direct sun and one in shade. The cube in the sun melts in minutes completely. The cube in the shade melts much slower today. Why does solar heat melt ice so rapidly? Heat energy breaks the solid bonds of ice crystals. Measure the melting times using clocks on desks! For instance, consider the example of Ice Test: Direct heat increases melting speed by breaking solid bonds. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Ice Test
examples.content: Direct heat increases melting speed by breaking solid bonds.

slides.title: Tinkering with Soil Textures
slides.text: We group rocks and soils by physical properties. Tinker with a pile of dry garden rocks. Feel their textures with your finger tips closely. Sort them into rough stones and smooth river pebbles. Why are river pebbles so smooth and rounded? Water flows over them, wearing down rough edges. Classifying rocks by texture is a science skill. Write down the rock groups in your log book! For instance, consider the example of Rock Test: River stones are smoothed by friction from flowing water. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Rock Test
examples.content: River stones are smoothed by friction from flowing water.

slides.title: Tinkering with Sprouting Seeds
slides.text: Seeds need specific conditions to start growing. Tinker with seeds placed in two soil pots. Keep one pot damp and one completely dry. Seeds in damp soil sprout green roots soon. Seeds in dry soil stay asleep and do not grow. Why do seeds need water to sprout roots? Water activates the seed embryo to start life. Record sprout heights in both pots daily! For instance, consider the example of Seed Test: Moisture triggers germination, ending the seed's rest state. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Seed Test
examples.content: Moisture triggers germination, ending the seed's rest state.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Forming Why Questions
slides.text: Let's practice forming scientific 'Why' questions. Look at a puddle of water drying up soon. Observe that heat from the Sun warms the water. Form the question: 'Why does heat cause evaporation?' This question asks about the cause of changes. It can be investigated by testing warm and cold water. Avoid questions like: 'Is puddle water happy?' Always search for causes when you ask why! For instance, consider the example of Why Question: Why does heat speed up the melting of solid butter? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Why Question
examples.content: Why does heat speed up the melting of solid butter?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Forming How Questions
slides.text: Let's practice forming scientific 'How' questions. Look at how green plants grow from dry seeds. Observe that plants grow roots and stems soon. Form: 'How does water help seeds break shells?' This question asks about the steps of processes. It can be answered by observing seeds under lenses. Avoid questions like: 'Do seeds dream in soil?' Always ask about processes when you ask how! For instance, consider the example of How Question: How does a magnet pull steel paperclips through paper? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: How Question
examples.content: How does a magnet pull steel paperclips through paper?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Squeezing Sponges
slides.text: Let's explore sponges in water like real scientists. Observe what happens when you squeeze a sponge. Squeeze it under water in a clear glass bowl. You will observe bubbles rising to the top. Why do bubbles rise when sponges are squeezed? The water enters the sponge, pushing air out. Air is lighter than water, so bubbles float up. Record this bubble observation in your journal page! For instance, consider the example of Observation: Squeezing releases trapped air, which floats up as bubbles. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Squeezing releases trapped air, which floats up as bubbles.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Water on Leaves
slides.text: Let's explore how water behaves on different leaves. Drip water drops on a shiny gabi leaf. Observe that water rolls off like small beads. Now drip water on a dry paper leaf on desk. Observe that water soaks in, wetting the paper. Why does water roll off gabi leaves completely? The waxy coating on the leaf blocks water absorption. This waxy property is waterproof, protecting the plant! For instance, consider the example of Observation: Waxy surfaces repel water, preventing absorption in leaves. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Waxy surfaces repel water, preventing absorption in leaves.

slides.title: Common Error: Vague Questions
slides.text: Avoid asking questions that cannot be tested. Scientists do not ask: 'Do balloons feel pain?' This question is vague and cannot be measured. Instead, ask: 'How does air amount affect balloon size?' This question can be measured using rulers easily. Always ask questions about physical properties and facts. Make sure your questions have measurable answers. Check your questions before you start experiments! For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Asking: 'Is clay happy when we mold it into shapes?' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Asking: 'Is clay happy when we mold it into shapes?'

slides.title: Common Error: Guessing Without Tests
slides.text: Do not just guess outcomes without performing tests. Tinkering and exploring are necessary to gather facts. Guessing that a key floats is not scientific. Place the key in water to verify if it sinks. Observations provide evidence to answer questions. Always test materials before drawing conclusions. Evidence makes scientific explanations reliable and solid. Never write down guesses as factual observations! For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Writing 'Stones float' in your log without testing them. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Writing 'Stones float' in your log without testing them.

slides.title: Summary of Week 2
slides.text: Congratulations! You completed all the slides for Week 2. Let's review the main science tools we mastered. We learned that curiosity drives scientific inquiry. We ask 'Why' and 'How' questions to start tests. We tinker with clay, sponges, and magnets to explore. We observe properties like floating, sinking, and melting. We avoid vague questions and test before guessing. You are now ready to take the quiz and challenges! For instance, consider the example of Key Takeaway: Tinkering with materials helps us form testable questions. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Takeaway
examples.content: Tinkering with materials helps us form testable questions.

standard.q: What do scientists do when they are curious about a material?
options: They ask questions and tinker with it.

standard.q: Which of the following is a good 'Why' question for an ice cube?
options: Why does ice melt when it gets warm?

standard.q: What happens to clay when you squeeze it in your hand?
Why does a balloon get bigger when you blow air into it?
options: Air takes up space and pushes outwards.

standard.q: Which of the following materials is waterproof (does not absorb water)?
What kind of question starts an exploration of magnets?
options: What materials can a magnet pull?

standard.q: What property are we testing when we drop a stone and a leaf in water?
How does sugar behave when mixed into warm water?
options: It dissolves and disappears from view.

standard.q: Which question helps us investigate shadows using a flashlight?
options: How does the distance of the light change the shadow's size?

standard.q: What happens to a spinning top when it rubs against a rough table?
What bounces off a mirror to show your reflection?
How can you make a stretched rubber band produce a higher sound?
In what direction do plant stems grow when placed near a sunny window?
options: Towards the light of the window
They do not grow at all.

standard.q: Where does an ice cube melt the fastest?
options: In a cup of cold water

standard.q: True or False: Asking 'How does water help seeds sprout?' is a scientific question.
What do we do when we 'tinker' with materials?
options: We read a storybook about them.
We touch, play, and experiment with them to see what happens.
We buy them from the market.
We throw them in the trash bin.

standard.q: Which of these objects will sink to the bottom of a water bucket?
Why does a sponge feel heavier after you dip it in water?
options: The sponge absorbs water into its holes.

standard.q: True or False: A scientist only asks questions that can be tested by observing or experimenting.
Which question is NOT scientific because it is about personal taste?
options: Does a paper plane fly further when folded thin?
Is science the most beautiful subject?
Does sugar dissolve faster in hot water?
Which materials are attracted to magnets?

standard.q: What happens to a dry sponge when you squeeze it under water?
options: Air bubbles escape and water goes inside.

standard.q: Why does water roll off a gabi leaf in beads?
options: The leaf is waxy and waterproof.

standard.q: Which VCV word represents a testable guess we form after asking questions?
True or False: Squeezing modeling clay changes its shape, but not its weight.
What do we call the result of our tinkering and testing?

challenge.q: If you observe a balloon getting smaller over three days, what is the best scientific question to ask?
options: Why does air slowly escape from the rubber balloon?
Does the balloon prefer being small?
Who drew a face on the balloon?

challenge.q: You tinker with a metal key and a plastic key in the sun. The metal key feels much hotter. Why?
options: Metal conducts and absorbs heat better than plastic.
The sun only shines on metal.

challenge.q: A student asks: 'Why do plants bend towards light?' How can we test this?
options: Put plants in a box with a single hole for light.
Sing songs to the plants in the dark.
Water the plants with colored juice.

challenge.q: Which of these is a testable question about magnets?
options: How many sheets of paper can a magnetic force pull through?
Why are magnets so cool to play with?

challenge.q: What can you infer when a dry sponge placed in oil does not release bubbles?
options: The sponge was already wet or oil behaves differently.

performance.title: Everyday Material Tinkering Log
performance.desc: Tinker with two materials at home (e.g., a plastic bottle cap and a steel spoon). Put them in water, place them in the sun, and touch them. Write two 'Why' or 'How' questions based on your observations, and explain what properties (floating, heat conduction) you tested to your parent.
labels: Conducted hands-on tinkering with two materials
Formulated two testable scientific questions (Why/How)
Correctly explained material properties based on tests

pages:
Exercise 1: Forming Inquiry Questions

Write a testable scientific question for each observation using Why or How.

1. Observation: An ice cube melts faster on a metal plate than a wood board.
Why does metal melt ice faster than wood?
2. Observation: Magnets pull steel paperclips but not copper pennies.
How do magnets attract paperclips but not pennies?

Exercise 2: Tinkering Observations

Write the correct observation result (Floats, Sinks, or Melts) for each activity.

1. Placing an iron nail in a cup of river water:
Sinks
2. Placing a dry bamboo twig on a quiet pond:
Floats
3. Placing solid butter on a warm pan:
Melts

Exercise 3: Clay and Elasticity Puzzles

Answer the questions using physical facts about clay and rubber bands.

Describe what happens to a rubber band when you stretch and release it:
It stretches when pulled and snaps back to its original shape.
This is because the rubber band has elastic properties.

slides.title: Welcome to the Animal Kingdom!
slides.text: Hello, Explorer! Welcome to English Week 2! This week, we are diving into the world of action. We will learn about words that start with double consonants. We will understand action verbs and how they show movement. We will ask questions like a scientist using question marks. We will also learn to read character feelings and traits. Let's keep our eyes open and watch animals in action. Are you ready to explore the animal kingdom today? For instance, consider the example of Weekly Goal: Master CCVC words, understand action verbs, ask question sentences, and infer feelings. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Weekly Goal
examples.content: Master CCVC words, understand action verbs, ask question sentences, and infer feelings.

slides.title: Starting Sounds (CCVC)
slides.text: CCVC words stand for Consonant-Consonant-Vowel-Consonant. They start with a blend of two consonant sounds. Think of the word f-r-o-g. The 'f' and 'r' blend. We say them together quickly: frog! CCVC words are different from CVCC words. They have their consonant blends at the beginning. Let's practice saying beginning consonant blends today. They help us name many interesting things in nature! For instance, consider the example of Word Pattern: C = Consonant, C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Word Pattern
examples.content: C = Consonant, C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant.

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: The Green Frog
slides.text: In the wet pond, we observe a green frog. The word 'frog' starts with the 'fr' blend. We watch how it leaps from leaf to leaf. We spell the word as f - r - o - g. Listen to the beginning sound of the word 'frog'. The letters 'f' and 'r' join to make 'fr'. It is a wonderful CCVC word we see in ponds. Let's say 'frog' aloud and feel the blend! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: f - r - o - g

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: The Crawling Crab
slides.text: On the sandy beach, we spot a small crab. It crawls sideways along the wet sand. The word 'crab' starts with the 'cr' blend. We spell the word as c - r - a - b. How many legs does a crab use to crawl? Crabs are fascinating creatures in the shoreline habitat. Say 'crab' to hear the 'cr' starting sound. It is another CCVC word in our nature list! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: c - r - a - b

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: In the Pond
slides.text: We watch a small fish swim in the water. The word 'swim' starts with the blend 'sw'. We must not block the drain plug in the sink. The word 'plug' starts with the blend 'pl'. Both 'swim' and 'plug' are CCVC spelling patterns. They help us describe actions and useful objects. We spell them as s-w-i-m and p-l-u-g. Practice writing these words in your notebook now! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: s - w - i - m and p - l - u - g To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: s - w - i - m  and  p - l - u - g

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Music and Play
slides.text: Hear the loud beat of the parade drum! The word 'drum' starts with the blend 'dr'. Red and blue flags wave in the blowing wind. The word 'flag' starts with the blend 'fl'. Both 'drum' and 'flag' add color to our day. They follow the CCVC word spelling structure. We spell them as d-r-u-m and f-l-a-g. Can you hear the starting sounds of drum and flag? For instance, consider the example of Spelling: d - r - u - m and f - l - a - g To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: d - r - u - m  and  f - l - a - g

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Safety First
slides.text: We see a red stop sign on the paved road. The word 'stop' starts with the blend 'st'. We must not slip on the wet bathroom floor. The word 'slip' starts with the blend 'sl'. Both 'stop' and 'slip' help keep us safe. They are common CCVC words used in instructions. We spell them as s-t-o-p and s-l-i-p. Be careful to say the starting consonants clearly! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: s - t - o - p and s - l - i - p To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: s - t - o - p  and  s - l - i - p

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Flat Leaves
slides.text: The leaves of the water lily are green and flat. The word 'flat' starts with the blend 'fl'. Be careful not to fall into a deep mud trap! The word 'trap' starts with the blend 'tr'. Both 'flat' and 'trap' describe physical things. They follow the CCVC word pattern in phonics. We spell them as f-l-a-t and t-r-a-p. Let's practice writing them on our paper today! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: f - l - a - t and t - r - a - p To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: f - l - a - t  and  t - r - a - p

slides.title: Grammar: Action Words (Verbs)
slides.text: Verbs are words that show action or movement. They tell us what people, animals, or things do. Without verbs, nothing in our stories would move! Verbs are the motor engines of our sentences. They show actions like run, jump, sing, and play. In science, we use verbs to describe natural events. E.g., 'The wind blows' or 'The water boils'. Let's find verbs in the sentences we read! For instance, consider the example of Key Concept: Verb = Action word To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Concept
examples.content: Verb = Action word

slides.title: Observing Animals in Action
slides.text: Let's watch animals in their natural habitats. The green frog leaps across the pond. The blue bird flies high in the clear sky. The gold fish swims in the cool stream. The action verbs are leaps, flies, and swims. They describe the movements of living things. Using specific verbs makes our observations clear. What actions can you observe a dog performing? For instance, consider the example of Action: leap, fly, swim, crawl, run To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Action
examples.content: leap, fly, swim, crawl, run

slides.title: Verbs in Our Daily Life
slides.text: We use verbs to talk about what we do. 'Matteo reads a story book in the room.' 'The children play at school during recess.' 'My sister writes a science report on paper.' The verbs are reads, play, and writes. They tell us the actions of our characters. Notice how the verb changes for one or many. Matteo reads (one), but the children play (many). To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: Matteo
examples.content: reads

examples.title: Children
examples.content: play

slides.title: Sentence Engine: Interrogative Sentences
slides.text: An interrogative sentence is an asking sentence. It asks a question to gather facts or details. It must always start with a capital letter. It must always end with a question mark (?). Scientists use interrogative sentences to investigate nature. They ask questions about how the world works. Let's learn how to ask questions in English. It is the first step to becoming a scientist! For instance, consider the example of Rule: Always start with a capital letter and end with a question mark. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Rule
examples.content: Always start with a capital letter and end with a question mark.

slides.title: Question Words
slides.text: We start questions with special interrogative words. These words are: Who, What, Where, When, and Why. 'Who is looking at the green frog in the pond?' 'What does the crawling crab eat on the beach?' 'Where does the water flow when it rains?' Each word asks for a different kind of detail. 'Where' asks for place, 'What' asks for things. Which question word asks about the reason why? For instance, consider the example of Question Words: Who, What, Where, When, Why To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Question Words
examples.content: Who, What, Where, When, Why

slides.title: Story Time: The Pond Detective
slides.text: Let's read a short story about Matteo's discovery: 'Matteo looks closely at the quiet pond. He asks, 'Who is hiding under the green leaf?' Suddenly, a green frog leaps out of the water! Matteo laughs and says, 'My frog friend is safe!'' This story has both telling and asking sentences. The question helps us find out what happens next. Let's look at the character's feelings next! For instance, consider the example of Reading Task: Identify the CCVC words and verbs in this story! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Reading Task
examples.content: Identify the CCVC words and verbs in this story!

slides.title: Inferring Feelings: Happy or Scared?
slides.text: To infer means to find clues in the story. We use these clues to understand what is not said. In our story, Matteo laughs when the frog leaps. He says, 'My frog friend is safe!' in a happy voice. We can infer that Matteo feels happy and relieved. He is not scared of the leaping green frog at all. Paying attention to actions helps us infer feelings. How do you feel when you see a frog leap? For instance, consider the example of Infer: Matteo laughs -> Matteo is happy. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Infer
examples.content: Matteo laughs -> Matteo is happy.

slides.title: Inferring Traits: Curious Matteo
slides.text: Character traits are the qualities of a person. We infer traits by looking at character choices. Matteo looks closely at the pond to find things. He asks questions about who is hiding under leaves. We can infer that Matteo is a curious boy. He acts like a scientist who loves to explore. Understanding traits makes story characters feel real. What traits do you show when you do experiments? For instance, consider the example of Infer: Asks questions -> Matteo is curious. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Infer
examples.content: Asks questions -> Matteo is curious.

slides.title: Grammar: Possessive Pronouns
slides.text: Possessive pronouns show who owns an object. They tell us who the object belongs to in a sentence. The pronouns are: my, your, his, her, and its. 'This is my magnifying glass.' (belongs to me). 'That is his science notebook.' (belongs to him). 'She plays with her green balloon.' (belongs to her). 'The dog wagged its tail.' (belongs to the dog). Let's practice showing ownership in our sentences! For instance, consider the example of Ownership: my book, his net, her drum To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Ownership
examples.content: my book, his net, her drum

slides.title: Interrogative vs Possessive
slides.text: Interrogative and possessive pronouns work together. We use an interrogative pronoun to ask a question: 'Whose science book is on the desk?' We use a possessive pronoun to give the answer: 'It is her science book on the desk.' This shows how questions and answers connect. Always use the correct pronoun for the character. Whose pencil are you using to write today? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: Interrogative
examples.content: Whose

examples.title: Possessive
examples.content: her

slides.title: Science Connection: Animal Habitats
slides.text: Where does the crab crawl during the day? It lives in its sandy shoreline habitat. The question 'Where does the crab live?' is interrogative. It ends with a question mark and asks about place. The answer is a declarative sentence telling a fact: 'The crab crawls in the wet sand.' We use verbs like 'crawls' to show its action. We use nouns like 'sand' to describe its habitat. For instance, consider the example of Observation: A crab crawling in the wet sand. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: A crab crawling in the wet sand.

slides.title: Science Connection: Water Safety
slides.text: Why must we keep the drain plug clean? A clean plug lets the water flow safely away. The question 'Why do we clean it?' is interrogative. The word 'flow' is an action verb for water. In science, we ask questions about water movement. We observe how liquid flows down the pipe. The word 'plug' is our CCVC spelling focus. We must spell it correctly as p - l - u - g! For instance, consider the example of Action Verb: flow, drain, clean To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Action Verb
examples.content: flow, drain, clean

slides.title: Guided Practice: Spotting CCVC
slides.text: Let's play a game to spot CCVC words. Read this: 'The child sat on a flat rock.' Which word starts with a double consonant blend? Yes, the word is 'flat'! It starts with 'fl'. The other words do not follow the CCVC pattern. Spelling 'flat' correctly requires the letters f-l-a-t. Can you find another CCVC word in your house? Look at the flag or the drain plug! For instance, consider the example of Check: f - l - a - t starts with the consonants f and l. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Check
examples.content: f - l - a - t starts with the consonants f and l.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Finding the Verb
slides.text: Let's practice finding action words in sentences. Read: 'The blue bird flies high in the sky.' What is the bird doing in the sentence? It is flying! So the verb is 'flies'. The other words are nouns, adjectives, or pronouns. The verb 'flies' tells us the action of the subject. Always look for the action word to find the verb. What verb describes what a fish does in water? For instance, consider the example of Verb Check: flies (action performed by the bird) To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Verb Check
examples.content: flies (action performed by the bird)

slides.title: Guided Practice: Question Mark
slides.text: Let's check the rules for asking sentences. Which sentence is punctuated correctly today? 'A. Where is the green frog.' or 'B. Where is the green frog?' B is correct because it asks a question! It must end with a question mark, not a period. Always check the ending mark of your questions. It tells the reader how to say the sentence! For instance, consider the example of Corrected: 'Where is the green frog?' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Corrected
examples.content: 'Where is the green frog?'

slides.title: Common Error: Capitalizing Pronouns
slides.text: A common mistake is capitalizing possessive pronouns. Do not write: 'This is My green frog.' Instead, write: 'This is my green frog.' Pronouns like 'my' or 'her' do not need capitals. They only start with a capital if they begin a sentence. E.g., 'My frog is green.' is correct. Keep pronouns lowercase when they are in the middle! Let's practice writing them correctly in our logs. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: 'This is My frog.' For instance, consider the example of Correct: 'This is my frog.' To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: 'This is My frog.'

examples.title: Correct
examples.content: 'This is my frog.'

slides.title: Summary of Week 2
slides.text: Congratulations! You completed all the slides for Week 2. Let's review the main language tools we mastered. We learned to decode CCVC words like frog, crab, and plug. We learned to identify action verbs in our sentences. We learned to write interrogative sentences with question marks. We learned to infer character feelings and traits from clues. We connected our English lessons to animal observations. You are now ready to take the quiz and challenges! For instance, consider the example of Vocabulary: frog, drum, plug, stop, crab, flag, slip, swim, flat, trap To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Vocabulary
examples.content: frog, drum, plug, stop, crab, flag, slip, swim, flat, trap

standard.q: Which word starts with a CCVC blend?
Identify the CCVC word in this sentence: 'The crab crawls.'

Which verb shows what fish do to move in water?

standard.q: What punctuation mark is used at the end of an interrogative sentence?
Complete the question: '___ is the color of the crab?'
Complete the question: '___ does the water flow when it rains?'
Which of these is an asking sentence that is punctuated correctly?
options: Who is hiding under the leaf?
who is hiding under the leaf.
Who is hiding under the leaf.
who is hiding under the leaf?

standard.q: Read: 'Matteo jumps and cheers when he sees the frog.' How does Matteo feel?
Read: 'The bird flew away when the cat came close.' What trait does the bird show?
If a character shares their lunch with a hungry classmate, we can infer they are:

options: To use clues to guess character qualities
To write a word down on paper
To count the number of characters

standard.q: Read: 'Matteo stays up to check on his plants.' We can infer that Matteo is:
Complete: 'Whose science book is this?' 'It is ___ book (belongs to me).'
Complete: 'The dog wagged ___ tail when it saw the water.'
Complete: '___ is looking at the pond?' 'Matteo is looking.'
Complete: 'She plays with ___ doll in the garden (belongs to her).'
Identify the interrogative pronoun in: 'What did you find in the soil?'

challenge.q: Challenge: Which sentence contains a CCVC word, an action verb, and a possessive pronoun?
options: The frog swims in my pond.
The dog barked at the cat.
Can you see the blue flag?

challenge.q: Challenge: If you want to ask a friend about the habitat of a crab, which question is correct?
options: The crab lives on the beach.

challenge.q: Challenge: Read: 'The frog sat still. It did not move even when a fly buzzed by. Soon, its head dropped.' What can we infer about the frog?
options: It is very tired or resting.
It is angry at the fly.

Challenge: Spot the error: 'whose green frog is that?'
