slides.title: Buod ng Ikalawang Linggo
slides.text: Binabati kita! Natapos mo ang lahat ng slide sa linggong ito. Natutunan natin ang pagbabahagi ng talento sa ating kapwa. Nalaman natin ang kahalagahan ng pagtuturo sa kalaro natin. Nag-aral tayong lumahok sa mga paligsahan sa paaralan. Iniwasan natin ang pakikipag-away at pagyayabang sa plaza. Gumawa tayo ng plano ng pagtulong sa ating pamilya ngayon. Handa ka na ba sa pagsusulit at hamon ng linggong ito? Ipagpatuloy ang pagiging disiplinadong bata sa GMRC natin! Halimbawa, tingnan natin ang tungkol sa Tandaan: Pagbabahagi = Pagmamahal. Lakas ng loob = Pagtalo sa hiya. Pagtulungang lagi! Upang maunawaan ang konseptong ito nang malalim, kailangan nating tingnan kung paano ito nalalapat sa iba't ibang sitwasyon sa ating pang-araw-araw na buhay. Sa pamamagitan ng pag-aaral ng paksang ito, ang mga mag-aaral ay maaaring bumuo ng mga kasanayan sa kritikal na pag-iisip.

examples.title: Tandaan
examples.content: Pagbabahagi = Pagmamahal. Lakas ng loob = Pagtalo sa hiya. Pagtulungang lagi!

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gawin sa iyong talento o kakayahan ayon sa GMRC?
options: itago ito sa baul
ibahagi ito upang makatulong sa kapwa
magyabang sa mga kalaro
huwag itong gamitin

standard.q: Paano mo matutulungan ang iyong kaklase na nahihirapan sa matematika?
options: pagtawanan ang kanyang mga mali
turuan siya nang may pasensya at linaw
isumbong siya sa principal
huwag siyang kausapin

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na asal kapag natalo ang iyong koponan sa laro sa plaza?
options: makipag-away sa kalaban
tanggapin ang resulta nang maayos at bumati sa nanalo
iyakan ang kapitan ng barangay
sabihing nadaya ka

standard.q: Paano mo mapagtatagumpayan ang pagkamahiyain sa pagsasalita sa harap ng klase?
options: magtago sa likod ng pintuan
magsanay magsalita sa harap ng salamin sa bahay
tumangging sumagot kailanman
umiyak sa harap ng guro

standard.q: Sino ang unang dapat nating pagbahagian ng ating mga talento at drawing?
options: ang ating sariling pamilya at magulang
mga pulis sa outpost
mga tindero sa palengke

standard.q: Ano ang tawag sa pag-uugnayan kung saan ang mga mag-aaral ay nagtutulungan sa klase?
options: pakikipagkapwa-tao at pagtutulungan

standard.q: Ano ang dapat mong sabihin sa kalaro na natatakot sumubok sumali sa patimpalak?
options: 'Huwag ka nang sumali dahil matatalo ka lang.'
'Kaya mo yan! Sumubok ka at mag-ensayo tayo.'
'Hindi maganda ang iyong talento.'
'Matulog ka na lang sa bahay.'

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang magandang gawi ng pagkusa sa tahanan?
options: maghintay ng utos bago mag-ayos ng higaan
kusang mag-ayos ng higaan paggising sa umaga
maglaro ng computer buong araw
itapon ang basura sa ilalim ng higaan

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na reaksyon kapag pinuri ng iyong magulang ang iyong iginuhit na larawan?
options: magyabang sa mga kapatid
magpasalamat nang may kababaang-loob at magpatuloy sa pagsasanay
itapon ang drawing sa basura

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pagbabahagi ng galing sa pagsusulat sa kalaro ay nagpapababa ng iyong sariling talento.
Alin sa mga sumusunod ang pambalana sa pook kung saan tayo maaaring magpatulong sa pagbasa ng aklat?
Sino ang nagtuturo sa atin ng disiplina sa paglalaro ng sports sa paaralan?
options: Guro o Coach

standard.q: Ano ang ibig sabihin ng pagiging 'sportsman' sa palaro sa plaza?
options: makipag-away kapag natalo
pagiging patas, disiplinado, at magalang sa kalaban
pagbili ng bagong bola
pagtatago ng sipa

standard.q: Punan ang patlang: 'Ang pagtulong sa gawaing-bahay ay nagpapakita ng ___ sa ating pamilya.'
options: pagmamahal at disiplina

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gawin kapag nakita mong nahihirapang magbasa ang iyong kalaro sa barangay?
options: pagtawanan siya sa kalsada
basahan siya ng kwento at turuan siya nang may pasensya
isumbong sa kanyang nanay
huwag siyang salihan sa laro

standard.q: Saan ginaganap ang mga opisyal na patimpalak ng katutubong awitin sa barangay?
options: sa barangay hall o plaza
sa loob ng health center
sa daan ng sasakyan

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pag-awit ng tradisyonal na awitin sa harap ng pamilya ay nakatutulong upang mabawasan ang kahiyaang sarili.
Aling ugali ang ipinapakita ng batang kusang nagdidilig ng mga halaman sa hardin?
options: pagkukusa at responsibilidad

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na salita kapag tinuturuan ang kalaro na magbilang ng barya?
options: 'Napakabagal mo namang matuto!'
'Dahan-dahan nating bilangin, kaya mo ito.'
'Huwag ka na lang mag-aral.'
'Akin na lang ang iyong barya.'

standard.q: Ano ang pangunahing layunin ng pakikipagkapwa sa klase?
options: makipag-away upang sumikat
magtulungan at magkaunawaan ang bawat mag-aaral
magpaligsahan sa tatak ng sapatos
magtago sa likod ng guro

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang tamang kilos kapag tinutulungan ang kapatid sa pagsulat?
options: sulatan ang kanyang papel para sa kanya
ganyakin siya at gabayan ang kanyang kamay nang may pasensya
gupitin ang kanyang papel
sabihing hindi siya matututo kailanman

standard.q: Tukuyin ang pambalana sa tao na nag-aalaga ng katawan sa health center:
options: doktor o nars

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pagbabahagi ng talento ay isang paraan ng pagpapasalamat sa biyayang ibinigay sa atin.
Alin sa mga sumusunod ang nagpapakita ng tamang lakas ng loob sa eskwela?
options: makipag-away sa mas malaking bata
sumali sa programa at umawit nang malinaw sa entablado
magtapon ng basura sa pasilyo
sumigaw sa loob ng aklatan

standard.q: Saan natin dapat ilapat ang ating mga natutunang mabubuting asal sa GMRC?
options: sa paaralan lamang
sa tahanan, paaralan, at sa buong komunidad araw-araw
tuwing may pagsusulit lamang
kapag nakatingin ang guro lamang

challenge.q: Challenge: Kung ang lahat ng mag-aaral sa Grade 3 ay tumangging ibahagi ang kanilang talento, ano ang maaaring mangyari sa paaralan?
options: Mas mabilis na matututo ang lahat.
Walang magiging pagtutulungan at mawawalan ng masayang programa ang eskwelahan.
Mas magiging sikat ang mga guro.
Walang magbabago sa klase.

challenge.q: Challenge: Paano nagkakaugnay ang disiplina sa paglalaro ng patintero sa kapayapaan ng barangay?
options: Ang disiplinado sa laro ay umiiwas sa away, na nagdudulot ng kapayapaan at pagkakaibigan sa mga kabataan sa komunidad.
Walang ugnayan ang dalawang ito.
Ang patintero ay nagpapasikat sa kapitan ng barangay.
Ang gulo sa laro ay nagpapasaya sa mga tanod.

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling paraan ang pinakamahusay upang tulungan ang isang kalaro na laging tinutukso dahil sa kanyang pagkakamali sa pag-awit?
options: tumawa rin kasama ang ibang bata
sabihan ang mga nanunukso na mali ang kanilang ginagawa, at sabay silang mag-ensayo ng kanta
sabihan siyang huwag na lang kumanta kailanman
isumbong sa pulis outpost agad

challenge.q: Challenge: Bakit ang pagkusa sa pagdidilig ng mga halaman sa bahay ay nagpapakita ng tiwala sa sarili ng isang Grade 3 student?
options: Dahil nagpapatunay ito na kaya niyang gampanan ang responsibilidad nang mag-isa nang hindi inuutusan.
Dahil ang mga halaman ay kailangang mamatay sa sun.
Dahil mas madali itong gawin kaysa sa math.
Dahil binabayaran siya ng kanyang nanay.

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling pag-uugnay ang pinakatumpak sa pagitan ng kababaang-loob at pagbabahagi ng talento?
options: Ibinabahagi ang galing upang maging sikat at mapuri ng lahat.
Ibinabahagi ang galing upang makatulong sa kapwa nang walang hinihintay na kapalit o pagyayabang.
Hindi ibinabahagi ang galing dahil baka may mas gumaling pa.
Ibinabahagi ang galing kapalit ng mga laruan.

performance.title: Aking Tsart ng Pagkusa
performance.desc: Gumawa ng isang Tsart ng Pagkusa sa isang malinis na papel. Ilista ang tatlong gawaing-bahay na gagawin mo araw-araw nang may pagkusa (hal. pag-aayos ng higaan, pagliligpit ng laruan, pagdidilig ng halaman). Lagyan ng checkmark ang bawat araw na nagawa mo ito. Ipakita ang tsart sa iyong magulang at hingin ang kanilang lagda bilang patunay.
labels: Maayos na naisulat ang tsart na may tatlong gawaing-bahay
Kusang isinagawa ang mga gawain nang may disiplina sa sarili
Maayos na naipaliwanag ang tsart at nakakuha ng lagda ng magulang

pages:
Pagsasanay 1: Pagbabahagi ng Talento

Tukuyin kung ang sitwasyon ay nagpapakita ng Pagbabahagi (pagtulong sa kapwa) o Pagyayabang (dapat iwasan) gamit ang stylus.

1. Tinuturuan ni Caloy ang kanyang kalaro na magbasa ng kwento sa hapon.
Pagbabahagi
2. Sinasabi ni Matteo sa lahat na siya lang ang magaling gumuhit sa buong klase.
Pagyayabang
3. Tinutulungan ni Lani ang kanyang kapatid na magkuwenta ng kanyang barya.
Pagbabahagi

Pagsasanay 2: Sportsmanship sa Palaro

Isulat ang tamang kilos (Batiin ang nanalo, Makipag-away, o Umiyak) kapag natalo sa sipa sa plaza.

1. Ang karapat-dapat na kilos ng isang sportsman sa komunidad:
Batiin ang nanalo
2. Ang maling kilos na nagdudulot ng gulo sa kalsada:
Makipag-away

Pagsasanay 3: Pagkusa sa Tahanan

Sumulat ng isang pangungusap upang ilarawan kung paano mo gagawin ang pagkusa sa inyong bahay paggising sa umaga.

Describe what you will do with your bed when you wake up:
Kusang aayusin ko ang aking higaan at itutupi ang aking kumot nang maayos.
Ito ay nagpapakita ng aking disiplina sa sarili nang hindi inuutusan ng aking nanay.

slides.title: Story: The Crow and the Pitcher - Part 1
slides.text: On a hot summer day, a very thirsty Crow flew all over the dry fields looking for water. The ground was baked hard, and the rivers were completely dry. For a very long time, she found nothing and felt like she would collapse from thirst. Just as she was about to lose hope, she spotted a large clay water pitcher sitting on a garden table. She flew down quickly and looked inside. There was indeed some clear water at the bottom, but the pitcher was deep and its neck was extremely narrow. No matter how she twisted her head, her beak could not reach the water. She tried to knock the pitcher over, but it was too heavy and did not budge at all. She was very frustrated and did not know what to do next. The Crow was extremely tired and felt her throat burning with thirst. She searched every corner of the garden, hoping to find a puddle or a water bowl, but the dry summer heat had evaporated every drop. When she saw the pitcher, she flew down with hope, but her disappointment was great when she realized she could not reach the water.

slides.title: Story: The Crow and the Pitcher - Part 2
slides.text: The Crow did not despair or give up. She stood still and looked around for a solution. She saw some small, smooth pebbles lying on the gravel path nearby. A clever idea came to her mind. She picked up one small pebble in her beak and carried it to the pitcher. She dropped the pebble inside. Then, she picked up another pebble and dropped it in. She kept doing this, walking back and forth, picking up pebbles one by one. It was hard, slow work, and she was very tired. But she did not stop. With each pebble she dropped into the pitcher, the water level at the bottom slowly rose a tiny bit higher. The pebbles were replacing the empty space inside, forcing the water upwards. She was patient and did not lose hope. She knew that if she worked steadily, she could find a way. So she walked back and forth, picking up the small stones and dropping them one by one into the pitcher. It was a long and exhausting task, but she kept going, showing that persistence is key to overcoming difficult obstacles.

slides.title: Story: The Crow and the Pitcher - Part 3
slides.text: She dropped twenty, thirty, forty pebbles into the pitcher. Gradually, the water level rose up the narrow neck of the clay container. Finally, the cool water reached the very top of the pitcher! The thirsty Crow dipped her beak in and drank happily until she was no longer thirsty. She felt refreshed and strong again. She chirped in joy and flew back into the sky. She proved that even a small bird can solve a big problem if she stays patient, works hard, and thinks creatively. The other birds watched in wonder. She showed everyone that determination and resourcefulness can help us overcome any difficult situation in our lives. We must never give up when things are hard. The water was cool and refreshing, and the Crow drank until she was fully satisfied. She felt her energy return and was ready to fly again. She proved that intelligence and hard work are more powerful than force. The other birds in the garden watched her and learned that patience and clever thinking can solve the hardest problems.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Pitcher - A container for holding and pouring liquids.
• Narrow - Thin or small width.
• Despair - Lose hope.
• Pebble - A small, smooth, round stone.

Observe how 'gravel' refers to a collection of tiny stones.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Why couldn't the Crow drink the water when she first found the pitcher?
2. What objects did the Crow drop into the pitcher?
3. What happened to the water level as more pebbles were added?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Resourcefulness and Problem Solving.

Necessity is the mother of invention. Where there is a will, there is a way. When we face a difficult challenge, thinking creatively and working step-by-step can help us find a solution.

Discuss: When was a time you solved a tricky problem by trying a different idea?

slides.title: Poem: The Starry Night - Part 1
slides.text:
The stars shine bright like diamonds in the sky,
As the silver moon goes sailing slowly by.
They blink their eyes and whisper from above,
Sending the sleeping world a song of love.

When the sun sets, the night sky transforms into a beautiful canvas of twinkling lights. Thousands of stars appear, shining like tiny lanterns in the darkness. The silver moon moves gracefully across the sky, casting a soft, gentle glow over the houses and trees below. It feels like the entire universe is singing a quiet, peaceful lullaby to help the world rest. The night is quiet, and the air is cool. I look up from my window and marvel at the vastness of the sky, wondering about the distant worlds shining so far away in the dark, blinking like little eyes in the deep space. twinkling like little eyes in the deep space. The night sky is a beautiful map of stars that has existed for billions of years. When we look up, we are seeing the light of stars that are very far away. It makes us feel small but also connected to the vast and wonderful universe that surrounds our Earth.

slides.title: Poem: The Starry Night - Part 2
slides.text:
The dark blue sky is like a blanket spread,
To keep us warm and safe inside our bed.
Until the golden sun begins to rise,
And wakes us up to open wide our eyes.

The deep blue sky stretches out above us, acting like a giant, soft blanket that wraps around the entire Earth. It makes me feel safe and protected as I sleep in my cozy bed. The twinkling stars seem to watch over us like guardians. But the night does not last forever. Soon, the dark sky begins to fade, replaced by soft pink and orange colors. The golden sun climbs slowly into the sky, sending warm rays of light to wake up the sleeping animals and children. It is time to start a new day of play and study. We open our eyes and welcome the bright morning light. We open our eyes and welcome the bright morning light. The sun brings warmth and light, helping plants grow and giving energy to all living things. The transition from the quiet, dark night to the active, bright day is a natural rhythm that repeats every single day, keeping our world balanced and healthy.

slides.title: Poem: The Starry Night - Part 3
slides.text:
We see the moon changes shape day by day,
Sometimes a crescent, sometimes round and gray.
But stars remain to guide us in the dark,
Twinkling above the quiet meadow and park.

The moon is a wonderful sight to watch. Some nights it looks like a thin, curved silver banana, which we call a crescent moon. On other nights, it is a big, round, bright circle. Even though the moon changes its shape, the stars are always there, constant and reliable. Long ago, sailors used the positions of the stars to find their way across the dark oceans. Today, we look at the same stars and dream of traveling into space. The night sky is a source of guide, beauty, science, and endless wonder for all of us. It makes our dreams fly high and inspires us to learn astronomy. It makes our dreams fly high and inspires us to learn astronomy. By studying the stars, we can learn about gravity, light, and the history of our solar system. The moon's phases also teach us about how the Earth and moon move around the sun. The night sky is a giant science classroom waiting for us to explore it.

slides.title: Rhyme & Metaphor
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: Rhyming Pairs & Similes
• Rhymes: sky / by, above / love, spread / bed, rise / eyes
• Simile: 'stars shine bright like diamonds' (comparing stars to diamonds using 'like').
• Simile: 'sky is like a blanket spread' (comparing the sky to a warm blanket).

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What are the stars compared to in the poem?
2. What is the moon compared to doing in the sky?
3. What color is the sun when it starts to rise?

slides.title: Moral Focus: Nature Appreciation
slides.text:
The natural world is full of beauty and peace. Taking a moment to look at the night sky helps us relax, appreciate the universe, and prepare for a restful sleep.

Discuss: What is your favorite thing to observe in nature during the day or night?

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 1
slides.text: Welcome back to our fun speech gym! Today we will visit a magical forest where brave brown bears live. These bears are very smart and hardworking. Brave brown bears build big wooden bridges. Big bridges are built by brave brown bears. The bears carry heavy logs to build bridges over the fast rivers. Let's try saying this together rapidly: 'Brave brown bears build big bridges. Big bridges are built by brave brown bears.' Make sure you make a strong /b/ sound by pressing your lips tightly together before releasing the air. Say it three times, starting slowly and then speeding up! It is a great way to train your mouth muscles for reading and speaking clearly in front of your classmates at school. To make a strong sound, we must practice saying these words slowly first. The bears worked together as a team, carrying the heavy logs and placing them carefully. They showed that cooperation and hard work can help us build strong structures and complete difficult tasks that we cannot do alone. It was a great lesson in teamwork.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 2
slides.text: Now, let's meet Danny and his playful pet. Danny has a dirty dog who loves to dig in the garden. Danny's dirty dog dug deep holes in the dirt. Did Danny's dirty dog dig deep holes? Yes, he did! He dug holes to hide his bones and toys. Let's practice our 'D' sounds: 'Danny's dirty dog dug deep holes. Did Danny's dirty dog dig deep holes?' To make a clean /d/ sound, place the tip of your tongue against the roof of your mouth, right behind your front teeth, and release the air with your voice. Try saying it three times without getting your tongue twisted. Polly the puppy watched Danny's dog and barked happily in the warm sun. It was a funny sight. To make a clean sound, practice repeating these words with your friends. The puppy loved to chase the dog around the yard, making a lot of noise. They had so much fun playing in the dirt under the shade of the big trees, showing that playing and exercising outdoors is good for our health and happiness.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 3
slides.text: Our final challenge is about Betty Botter, a baker who bought some butter. Betty Botter bought some fresh butter. But, she said, this butter's bitter! If I put it in my batter, it will make my batter bitter. But a bit of better butter will make my batter better. So she bought a bit of butter, better than her bitter butter, and she baked it in her batter, and her batter was not bitter! Let's also bake big batches of brown bread. Brown bread baked in big batches by the bears. Let's say: 'Bake big batches of brown bread.' Say it as fast as you can! Practicing these tongue twisters daily makes your speech very clear, helpful, and confident. You will speak like a professional speaker. Practicing these tongue twisters daily is a wonderful way to improve our pronunciation and build speaking confidence. It helps us speak clearly when we read stories, answer questions in class, or talk to our friends. Remember to breathe and speak slowly at first, then increase your speed as you get better.

slides.title: Speech & Pronunciation Practice
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: Contrasting /b/ and /d/
• /b/ (Bilabial): Close both lips together, then release air with voice (e.g., 'bears', 'bridges', 'bread').
• /d/ (Alveolar): Place the tip of your tongue against the roof of your mouth behind your teeth, then release air with voice (e.g., 'Danny', 'dog', 'dug').

Practicing these keeps our tongue and lips nimble!

slides.title: Comprehension & Speech Tasks
slides.text:
Tasks:
1. Read Twister 1 and Twister 4 aloud three times, increasing speed.
2. Say Twister 2 without letting your tongue slip on 'd'.
3. What did Betty Botter buy, and what was wrong with it?

slides.title: Focus: Practice & Focus
slides.text:
Tongue twisters help improve reading speed and speech clarity. Practice daily to train your speech muscles and build confidence.

Discuss: Why do news announcers and actors practice tongue twisters before going on air?

slides.title: Biography: Thomas Edison - Part 1
slides.text: As a young boy in Ohio, USA, Thomas Edison was very curious but had many difficulties. He developed hearing problems after an illness, making it hard for him to listen. In school, his teacher thought he was too slow to learn because he asked too many questions instead of copying notes. Seeing this, his loving mother took him out of school and decided to teach him at home. Thomas loved reading science and history books. He was fascinated by chemical experiments. He saved his pocket money to buy jars, tubes, and chemicals, and set up his very first chemistry laboratory in the cellar of his house. He spent all his free time experimenting there, wanting to discover new facts about nature. Thomas's mother was very patient and encouraging. She believed in her son's abilities and helped him study science, history, and math. She allowed him to explore his interests and conduct experiments, which helped him develop his curiosity and love for learning. He learned that asking questions is the first step to making new discoveries.

slides.title: Biography: Thomas Edison - Part 2
slides.text: When he grew up, Thomas became a full-time inventor, creating a famous laboratory in Menlo Park, New Jersey. He wanted to invent a safe, clean, and cheap electric light bulb to replace dirty oil lamps. He knew it was possible, but he needed to find the right material for the filament—the thin wire inside the bulb that glows when heated. He and his team tested thousands of different materials, including metal wires, paper, plants, and even hair! None of them lasted long enough; they all burned up in a few minutes. People laughed at him and said he was failing. But Thomas remained patient and focused on his goal, never letting the jokes bother him. Thomas and his team worked long hours in the laboratory, testing different types of materials. They did not let the failures discourage them. They knew that each failed test brought them closer to finding the right material. This showed that scientific invention requires a lot of patience, hard work, and determination.

slides.title: Biography: Thomas Edison - Part 3
slides.text: Thomas Edison did not give up. When a reporter asked him about his failures, he famously said, 'I have not failed. I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work.' Eventually, after testing over six thousand materials, he found that a carbonized bamboo thread worked perfectly, glowing for over forty hours! This was the first practical electric light bulb. Edison went on to invent many other amazing things, including the phonograph (the first record player) and the motion picture camera for movies. He held over one thousand patents! He proved that resilience, curiosity, and hard work are the keys to scientific discovery and changing the world for the better. He is a great example of dedication. Edison's inventions changed the way people lived all over the world. His electric light bulb allowed people to work, study, and play safely at night, while his phonograph brought music into people's homes. He showed that anyone can be an inventor if they have a curious mind, work hard, and never give up when faced with challenges.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Cellar - An underground room, often used for storage.
• Inventor - A person who creates new things or processes.
• Filament - A thin wire inside a light bulb that glows when heated.
• Bamboo - A fast-growing woody grass.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Who taught Thomas Edison at home after he struggled in school?
2. What did Edison say when people told him he was failing to make a light bulb?
3. Name two other inventions Edison created besides the electric light bulb.

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Resilience and Learning from Failure.

Failure is not the opposite of success; it is a step along the way. Every mistake we make teaches us what doesn't work, helping us get closer to what does work. Keep trying!

Discuss: When is a time you made a mistake on a school task, and what did you learn from it?

slides.title: Fable: The Wind and the Sun - Part 1
slides.text: The North Wind and the Sun had a big argument about who was the stronger of the two. They argued for days, each claiming they had more power over nature. To settle the dispute, they agreed to test their strength on a traveler who was walking down a dusty road. Whoever could force the traveler to take off his warm, heavy cloak would be declared the winner. The North Wind took his turn first. He gathered all his air and blew as hard as he could. He whistled through the trees, howled over the hills, and sent cold blasts of air straight at the traveler, trying to rip the cloak away by force. But the traveler did not let go. The Wind believed that force and power were the only ways to show strength. He wanted to prove that he could control everything with his strong blasts. But he did not realize that true strength is not about being loud or violent, but about having a gentle and positive influence on others.

slides.title: Fable: The Wind and the Sun - Part 2
slides.text: However, the harder the Wind blew and the colder the air became, the tighter the traveler wrapped his heavy cloak around his shoulders. He held onto it with both hands, shivering and walking slowly. The Wind blew and blew until he was completely tired out. Disappointed and weak, the Wind gave up his turn. Then, it was the Sun's turn. The Sun did not use force or noise. He simply shone warmly and gently through the clouds. His warm rays filled the air and touched the traveler's face. Soon, the traveler stopped shivering. He felt the pleasant warmth of the sun and unbuttoned his cloak, feeling the calm breeze. The Sun's warmth was gentle and pleasant, making the traveler feel comfortable and relaxed. The traveler did not feel threatened or forced, so he naturally decided to adjust his clothing. This showed that gentleness can make people cooperate willingly, while force only makes them resist and hold on tighter.

slides.title: Fable: The Wind and the Sun - Part 3
slides.text: The Sun continued to shine, getting a little warmer and brighter. The day became hot, and the air was calm. The traveler began to sweat under the heavy wool cloak. Finally, he could stand the heat no longer. He took off his cloak completely, threw it over his shoulder, and ran to sit under the cool shade of a tree by the road. The Sun had won the contest easily without any noise or violence! The Wind watched in silence. The Sun proved that gentleness, warmth, and kind persuasion can achieve much more than force, anger, and harsh demands. We can apply this lesson in our daily lives when talking to others and resolving disagreements. The Wind apologized to the Sun and admitted that he was wrong. He learned that being gentle and kind is much more effective than being angry and forceful. This story teaches us to treat others with kindness and respect, using polite words and understanding to resolve our problems and work together peacefully. Gentleness always succeeds where force fails.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Settle - Resolve or reach an agreement.
• Traveler - A person who is traveling.
• Cloak - A loose outer garment or coat.
• Howling - Making a loud, wailing noise (like wind or animals).

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What was the contest between the Wind and the Sun?
2. What did the traveler do when the Wind blew harder?
3. How did the Sun get the traveler to take off his cloak?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Gentleness and Persuasion.

Gentleness and kind persuasion achieve more than force and anger. We can often convince others to cooperate by being warm, polite, and helpful rather than using harsh demands.

Discuss: How can you use kind words to ask a friend to share a toy or game?

slides.title: Kuwento: Si Malakas at si Maganda - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Noong unang panahon, wala pang lupa at tao sa mundo. Ang mayroon lamang ay ang asul na langit, ang malawak na karagatan, at isang makapangyarihang ibon na lumilipad sa pagitan nito. Isang araw, naging pagod ang ibon sa paglipad at nagpasya na lumikha ng mga isla upang may masilungan. Nang magkaroon ng mga pulo, tumubo ang iba't ibang halaman, kabilang ang isang napakalaking puno ng kawayan sa pampang. Habang namamasyal ang ibon sa tabi ng mga kawayan, narinig niya ang isang kakaibang katok mula sa loob ng isang malaking kawayan. 'Tok! Tok! Tok!' Parang may humihingi ng tulong mula sa loob nito.

slides.title: Kuwento: Si Malakas at si Maganda - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Dahil sa kuryusidad, tinuka ng ibon ang makapal na kawayan hanggang sa mabiyak ito sa gitna. Laking gulat ng ibon nang lumabas ang dalawang magagandang nilalang mula sa loob ng kawayan. Ang una ay isang lalaki na matikas, matapang, at may malalaking kalamnan. Tinawag niya ang kanyang sarili na si Malakas. Ang pangalawa ay isang napakagandang babae na may mahabang buhok, mayumi, at mahinhin kumilos. Tinawag niya ang kanyang sarili na si Maganda. Sila ang mga kauna-unahang tao na nanirahan sa mga isla ng Pilipinas. Pinakita nila sa ibon ang kanilang pasasalamat sa pagpapalaya sa kanila sa madilim na kawayan.

slides.title: Kuwento: Si Malakas at si Maganda - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Nagtulungan sina Malakas at Maganda upang magtayo ng kanilang tirahan at maghanap ng pagkain. Si Malakas ang nagputol ng mga puno at nagtayo ng matibay na kubo upang maprotektahan sila sa bagyo at init, habang si Maganda naman ang nag-alaga sa kanilang bakuran at nagtanim ng mga halamang-gamot. Nagsimula silang magkaroon ng pamilya at nagturo sa kanilang mga anak ng kahalagahan ng paggawa, respeto, at pagkakaisa sa komunidad. Sila ang pinagmulan ng lahing Pilipino, na kilala sa pagiging matatag tulad ni Malakas at mapagmahal sa sining at kapayapaan tulad ni Maganda.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Matikas - Malakas ang pangangatawan at may magandang tindig. • Kawayan - Isang matibay na uri ng damo na parang kahoy na ginagamit sa pagtatayo ng bahay. • Mayumi - Mahinhin o magandang kumilos. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'm' sa 'malakas', 'maganda', at 'mundo'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Saan nagmula sina Malakas at Maganda ayon sa alamat? 2. Ano ang mga katangian ni Malakas at Maganda? 3. Paano sila nagtulungan sa kanilang bagong tahanan?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang pagkakaisa ng lakas at kagandahan ay nagdudulot ng magandang komunidad. Ang bawat tao ay may kani-kaniyang talento at lakas na dapat gamitin para sa kabutihan ng pamilya. Ang pagtutulungan ng bawat kasapi ng pamilya ay susi sa masayang tahanan. Discuss: Ano ang iyong sariling lakas na maaari mong ibahagi upang makatulong sa pamilya?

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Kuneho at ang Pagong - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Isang umaga sa kagubatan, nagyabang si Kuneho sa kanyang bilis sa harap ng iba pang mga hayop. 'Walang sinuman sa kagubatan ang makakatalo sa akin sa takbuhan!' sigaw niya habang tumatalon-talon. Nang marinig ito ni Pagong, lumapit siya nang dahan-dahan at sinabi, 'Tinatanggap ko ang iyong hamon, Kuneho. Magpaligsahan tayo sa susunod na Linggo.' Tumawa nang malakas si Kuneho at sinabing, 'Nababaliw ka ba, Pagong? Ang bagal mo kayang maglakad!' Ngunit pumayag pa rin si Kuneho dahil nais niyang ipakita sa lahat ang kanyang bilis. Nagtakda sila ng ruta sa kagubatan patungo sa tuktok ng bundok.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Kuneho at ang Pagong - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Nagsimula ang karera sa hudyat ng kuwago. Agad na tumakbo nang napakabilis si Kuneho at nawala sa paningin ng mga hayop. Samantala, si Pagong naman ay nagsimulang maglakad nang dahan-dahan, hakbang-hakbang nang walang hinto. Nang malapit na si Kuneho sa dulo, lumingon siya at nakitang napakalayo pa ni Pagong. 'Matutulog muna ako sa ilalim ng punong ito dahil napakabagal ni Pagong,' sabi ni Kuneho sa sarili. Humiga si Kuneho sa malambot na damo at mabilis na nakatulog dahil sa hanging malamig. Samantala, patuloy pa rin sa paglalakad si Pagong sa ilalim ng mainit na araw.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Kuneho at ang Pagong - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Lumipas ang ilang oras. Nagpatuloy si Pagong sa paglalakad nang walang tigil hanggang sa malagpasan niya ang natutulog na si Kuneho. Nang magising si Kuneho, nagulat siya nang makitang malapit na si Pagong sa dulo ng karera. Tumakbo si Kuneho nang buong lakas, ngunit huli na ang lahat! Naabot na ni Pagong ang linya ng tagumpay at sumigaw sa tuwa ang mga hayop. Nahiya si Kuneho sa kanyang kayabangan at humingi ng paumanhin kay Pagong. Napatunayan ni Pagong na ang tiyaga at determinasyon ay mas mahalaga kaysa sa bilis na walang pagpapakumbaba.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Nagyabang - Nagmalaki o ipinakita ang sariling galing nang may kasamang pagmamataas. • Ruta - Daanan o direksyon ng paglalakbay. • Determinasyon - Katatagan ng loob upang maabot ang layunin. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'k' sa 'kuneho', 'karera', at 'kagubatan'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Bakit tinanggap ni Pagong ang hamon ni Kuneho kahit alam niyang mabagal siya? 2. Ano ang ginawa ni Kuneho habang tumatakbo sa karera? 3. Sino ang nanalo sa paligsahan, at paano niya ito nagawa?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang tiyaga at pagpapakumbaba ay susi sa tagumpay. Huwag maliitin ang kakayahan ng iba kahit sa tingin mo ay mas magaling ka. Ang patuloy na pagsisikap nang may katapatan ay nagdadala ng tunay na panalo. Talakayin: Ano ang gagawin mo kapag nahihirapan ka sa isang aralin sa paaralan?

math.title: Mathematics
math.subtitle: Week 2: Metric Area and Geometry Basics

slides.title: Standard Units of Area
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Let's learn about standard metric units for area. Instead of using arbitrary tiles, we use standard square centimeters and square meters to measure flat surfaces accurately. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area units = standard measurements. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why do we need standard units instead of using any size of tile?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area units = standard measurements.

slides.title: The Square Centimeter
slides.text: Advanced analysis: A square centimeter is a metric unit of area. It represents the space covered by a tiny square where each of the four sides is exactly one centimeter long. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: 1 sq. cm = 1 cm x 1 cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you name three small objects you can measure in square centimeters?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: 1 sq. cm = 1 cm x 1 cm.

slides.title: Measuring in Square Centimeters
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We use square centimeters to measure small surfaces like books, index cards, or smartphones. We multiply the length and width in centimeters to find the area. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = 6 cm x 4 cm = 24 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of a photo that is 10 cm long and 8 cm wide?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = 6 cm x 4 cm = 24 sq. cm.

slides.title: The Square Meter
slides.text: Advanced analysis: A square meter is a larger metric unit of area. It is the space covered by a large square where each of the four sides is exactly one meter long. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: 1 sq. m = 1 m x 1 m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Would you measure the floor of your classroom in centimeters or meters?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: 1 sq. m = 1 m x 1 m.

slides.title: Measuring in Square Meters
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We use square meters to measure larger surfaces like room floors, gardens, or school blackboards. We multiply the length and width in meters to find the area. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = 5 m x 4 m = 20 sq. m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of a rectangular garden that is 6 m long and 4 m wide?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = 5 m x 4 m = 20 sq. m.

slides.title: Estimating Metric Area
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We can estimate metric areas by comparing them to familiar objects. For example, a student desk is about one-half of a square meter in surface area. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Estimate = close guess of area in metric units. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How can you estimate the floor area of your bedroom without a ruler?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Estimate = close guess of area in metric units.

slides.title: Comparing Area Units
slides.text: Advanced analysis: It is important to choose the right unit. Measuring a large room in square centimeters would give a huge number, while a notebook in square meters is too small. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: 1 sq. m = 10,000 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why is it better to measure a school playground in square meters?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: 1 sq. m = 10,000 sq. cm.

slides.title: Drawing a Metric Square
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Let's draw a square with a side of 3 centimeters using a ruler. We draw four equal sides of 3 cm, meeting at perfect square corners. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = 3 cm x 3 cm = 9 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of the square you just drew on your paper?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = 3 cm x 3 cm = 9 sq. cm.

slides.title: Drawing a Metric Rectangle
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Let's draw a rectangle with a length of 5 centimeters and a width of 3 centimeters. We draw opposite sides equal and straight using our ruler. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = 5 cm x 3 cm = 15 sq. cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you calculate the area of the drawn rectangle in square centimeters?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = 5 cm x 3 cm = 15 sq. cm.

slides.title: Introducing Geometry: The Point
slides.text: Advanced analysis: In geometry, a point represents an exact position or location. We draw it as a small dot and name it using a single capital letter, like Point A. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Point = exact location in space. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you find a point on a map showing where your school is?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Point = exact location in space.

slides.title: Points in the Real World
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We see models of points everywhere. A tiny pin on a digital map, a dot on a paper, or a star in the night sky all represent geometric points. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Dot = Point location. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How does a point help us mark a precise location on a grid?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Dot = Point location.

slides.title: Introducing the Line
slides.text: Advanced analysis: A geometric line is a straight path of points that extends forever in opposite directions. To show it has no end, we draw arrowheads on both ends. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Line = straight path that goes on forever. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How do the two arrowheads show that a line never stops going?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Line = straight path that goes on forever.

slides.title: Representing Lines
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We name a line by picking two points on it, like A and B, and writing a small line symbol with two arrows above the letters, like Line AB. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Line AB = straight path through A and B. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you draw a straight line and label two points on it?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Line AB = straight path through A and B.

slides.title: The Line Segment
slides.text: Advanced analysis: A line segment is a part of a line that has two fixed endpoints. It has a definite length, which means we can measure it using a metric ruler. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Segment AB = path between endpoints A and B. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why can we measure a line segment but not a whole line?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Segment AB = path between endpoints A and B.

slides.title: Measuring Line Segments
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We measure a line segment by placing the zero mark of our ruler at one endpoint and reading the number at the other endpoint of the segment. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Length = 8 cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the length of a pencil segment when you measure it?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Length = 8 cm.

slides.title: Introducing the Ray
slides.text: Advanced analysis: A ray is a part of a line that starts at one endpoint and extends forever in only one direction. We show this with one arrowhead at the end. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Ray AB = starts at A, goes forever through B. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How does a ray differ from a line and a line segment?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Ray AB = starts at A, goes forever through B.

slides.title: Rays in Daily Life
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We see models of rays in our daily environment. A beam of light coming from a flashlight or sunlight shining down on Mount Matutum represents a ray. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Sunbeam = model of a geometric ray. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why does a flashlight beam act like a ray in geometry?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Sunbeam = model of a geometric ray.

slides.title: Comparing Segments and Rays
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Let's compare: a line segment has two endpoints and can be measured. A ray has only one endpoint and goes on forever, so it cannot be measured. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Segment = 2 endpoints, Ray = 1 endpoint. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Which of these two has a definite length that you can write down?

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Segment = 2 endpoints, Ray = 1 endpoint.

slides.title: T'nalak Weaving Patterns
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Weavers in Lake Sebu create beautiful geometric patterns. They use straight lines and points in their designs to make beautiful traditional T'nalak cloths. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Geometric designs = points and segments. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. A straight line goes on forever in opposite directions, which we show with two arrowheads. Can you spot straight line segments in traditional hand-woven mats?

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Geometric designs = points and segments.

slides.title: City Layout Maps
slides.text: Advanced analysis: City planners in Koronadal City use points and lines to design maps. Points show buildings, and lines show streets that connect different places in the town. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Street layout = lines and intersections. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How do points and lines make maps easier for people to read?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Street layout = lines and intersections.

slides.title: Fish Cage Grids in Lake Sebu
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Fishermen in Lake Sebu layout rectangular fish cages in the lake water. They measure the area in square meters to decide how many tilapia fish to raise. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = 10 m x 10 m = 100 sq. m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. If a fish cage is 10 meters on each side, what is area?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = 10 m x 10 m = 100 sq. m.

slides.title: Drawing Parallel Paths
slides.text: Advanced analysis: We can draw two straight paths that stay the same distance apart using our ruler. These paths show us how lines can run side-by-side without crossing. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Distance = constant between straight paths. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Can you draw two straight paths that never meet on your paper?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Distance = constant between straight paths.

slides.title: Geometry Tools in Action
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Using the right tools is important in geometry. A sharp pencil and a flat ruler help us draw clean points, straight lines, and precise line segments. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Ruler + Pencil = precise geometric drawings. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. Why is a flat ruler necessary when drawing geometric figures?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Ruler + Pencil = precise geometric drawings.

slides.title: Solving Metric Area Problems
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Let's solve an area problem. A rectangular classroom floor is 8 meters long and 5 meters wide. We multiply 8 by 5 to find the floor area. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = 8 m x 5 m = 40 sq. m. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. What is the area of a notebook that is 15 cm by 10 cm?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = 8 m x 5 m = 40 sq. m.

slides.title: Summary of Week 2 Lessons
slides.text: Advanced analysis: Let's review our lessons. We learned about standard area units (sq. cm and sq. m), drawing shapes, and recognizing basic geometric elements: points, lines, segments, and rays. In math, we express this as: Advanced math: Area = metric space, Geometry = points and lines. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. A square centimeter (sq. cm) is a standard metric unit used for measuring small areas. A square meter (sq. m) is a larger metric unit, perfect for floors, gardens, and classrooms. We can convert between metric units by knowing that one square meter contains ten thousand square centimeters. In geometry, a point is represented by a dot and labeled with a capital letter like A or B. How do geometry and area help us describe the physical world?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Advanced math: Area = metric space, Geometry = points and lines.

pages:
Exercise 1: Metric Area Problems

Calculate the area using the formula and write your answer with the correct unit (sq. cm or sq. m).

1. A rectangular tuna packing box in GenSan is 80 cm long and 50 cm wide. What is its area?
4000 sq. cm

Exercise 2: Identifying Geometry Figures

Look at the drawings and write whether it is a Point, Line, Line Segment, or Ray.

1. A straight path with arrowheads on both ends:
Line
2. A straight path with two solid endpoints:
Line Segment

Exercise 3: Geometry Applications

Write the name of the real-world model represented by each geometric concept.

1. A beam of light coming from a flashlight:
Ray

performance.title: [Advanced] Geometry and Area Map of a Park
performance.desc: Advanced Task: Design a small park layout in Koronadal City on paper. Draw a rectangular playground that is 10 cm long and 8 cm wide, and calculate its area. Near the playground, draw a straight walking path (representing a line segment), a streetlamp shining light downwards (representing a ray), and a fountain marker (representing a point). Label all figures clearly, take a photo, and upload it.
labels: Playground area calculation (80 sq. cm)
Correct labels for Point, Segment, and Ray
Clean drawing design

science.title: Science
science.subtitle: Week 2: Asking Questions Like a Scientist

slides.title: Formulating Scientific Questions
slides.text: An advanced scientific question must isolate variables and be testable through empirical observation and controlled experimentation. For instance, consider the example of Empirical: Based on verifiable observations or experience rather than theory. For instance, consider the example of Isolating Variables: Ensuring only one factor is changed to observe its direct effect. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Empirical
examples.content: Based on verifiable observations or experience rather than theory.

examples.title: Isolating Variables
examples.content: Ensuring only one factor is changed to observe its direct effect.

slides.title: Inquiry and Hypotheses
slides.text: Questions drive hypotheses. A hypothesis acts as a bridge between a question and an experimental test. For instance, consider the example of Formulating: Converting 'Why does this happen?' into 'If we change X, then Y will behave in this way.' For instance, consider the example of Operational Definition: Defining exactly how a variable will be measured. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Formulating
examples.content: Converting 'Why does this happen?' into 'If we change X, then Y will behave in this way.'

examples.title: Operational Definition
examples.content: Defining exactly how a variable will be measured.

slides.title: Defining Testable Parameters
slides.text: Vague concepts like 'better', 'faster', or 'stronger' must be defined using measurable parameters (time, length, mass). For instance, consider the example of Subjective: Example: 'Which plane is best?' (Not testable). For instance, consider the example of Objective: Example: 'Which plane design has the longest flight time in seconds?' (Testable). To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Subjective
examples.content: Example: 'Which plane is best?' (Not testable).

examples.title: Objective
examples.content: Example: 'Which plane design has the longest flight time in seconds?' (Testable).

slides.title: Tinkering with Viscosity
slides.text: Tinkering with different liquids (water, oil, honey) shows how liquid thickness affects flow rate. For instance, consider the example of Viscosity: A fluid's resistance to flow. Honey has higher viscosity than water. For instance, consider the example of Experiment: How does heating honey affect its flow rate down a cardboard ramp? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Viscosity
examples.content: A fluid's resistance to flow. Honey has higher viscosity than water.

examples.title: Experiment
examples.content: How does heating honey affect its flow rate down a cardboard ramp?

slides.title: Tinkering with Air Pressure
slides.text: Tinkering with syringes and plastic tubes shows how gas compression behaves under force. For instance, consider the example of Compression: Squeezing air decreases its volume while increasing pressure. For instance, consider the example of Pneumatics: Using compressed gas to transmit force and perform mechanical work. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Compression
examples.content: Squeezing air decreases its volume while increasing pressure.

examples.title: Pneumatics
examples.content: Using compressed gas to transmit force and perform mechanical work.

slides.title: Tinkering with Capillary Action
slides.text: Tinkering with paper towels and colored water shows how liquids climb upwards against gravity. For instance, consider the example of Capillary Action: Liquid moving through tiny spaces due to adhesion and cohesion. For instance, consider the example of Application: How does paper towel thickness affect the rate of water absorption? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Capillary Action
examples.content: Liquid moving through tiny spaces due to adhesion and cohesion.

examples.title: Application
examples.content: How does paper towel thickness affect the rate of water absorption?

slides.title: Tinkering with Electromagnetism
slides.text: Tinkering with copper wire, an iron nail, and a battery creates a temporary magnet. For instance, consider the example of Electromagnet: A magnet created by running electric current through a coil of wire. For instance, consider the example of Variables: How does the number of wire coils affect the number of paperclips pulled? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. examples.title: Variables

slides.title: Tinkering with Aerodynamic Lift

slides.title: Common Error: Confounding Variables
slides.text: Changing more than one variable at a time makes it impossible to know which caused the result. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Testing different plane shapes using different paper materials. For instance, consider the example of Correct: Using the exact same paper material for all plane shapes tested. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. slides.title: Common Error: Ignoring Anomalies
slides.text: Do not ignore unexpected data points. Anomalies often lead to new discoveries. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Deleting a test result because it does not fit your hypothesis. For instance, consider the example of Correct: Recording the anomaly and investigating what hidden variable caused it. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. standard.q: Which of the following is the most testable scientific question?
options: Does heating honey reduce the time it takes to flow down a 30-degree ramp?

standard.q: What do we call the factor in an experiment that is intentionally changed by the scientist?
Why must we keep controlled variables constant in an experiment?
options: To make the experiment run faster.
To ensure that any changes in the dependent variable are caused ONLY by the independent variable.
To test multiple hypotheses at the same time.
To get a high score on the test.

standard.q: What property is demonstrated when water climbs up a paper towel suspended over a cup?
How does adding liquid dish soap to water affect its surface tension?
options: It reduces it, breaking the cohesive forces.

standard.q: A student wants to test if the number of coils in an electromagnet affects its strength. What is the dependent variable?
options: The number of coils wound around the nail

standard.q: Why does a raw egg spin slower and stop sooner than a hard-boiled egg?
options: The liquid inside the raw egg sloshes, creating internal friction (inertia) that opposes rotation.
The shell of a raw egg is rougher.
Hard-boiled eggs have more magic force.

standard.q: Which of the following describes Bernoulli's principle?
options: As fluid velocity increases, static pressure decreases.
Objects float if they displace equal water weight.
Action has an equal and opposite reaction.
Heat flows from hot to cold objects.

standard.q: In a plant shoe-box maze experiment testing phototropism, what is the independent variable?
options: The amount of light entering the box

standard.q: Which method is used to measure the volume of an irregular solid object like a stone?
options: Using a ruler to measure width
Water displacement in a graduated cylinder
Weighing it on a digital scale

standard.q: What is the angle of reflection if light hits a flat mirror at an angle of 30 degrees?
True or False: Viscosity is a liquid's resistance to flow.
What is a control group in a scientific experiment?
options: The group that controls the equipment.
The group kept under standard conditions to act as a baseline comparison.
The group where all variables are changed.

standard.q: Which variable is changed when testing the effect of salt concentration on water's boiling point?
What happens to water molecules at the surface to create surface tension?
options: They pull tightly towards each other due to cohesive forces.

standard.q: What hormone in plants is responsible for cell elongation on the dark side of a stem during phototropism?
Which material has the highest thermal conductivity?
How does increasing the frequency of a sound wave affect its pitch?
True or False: Capillary action occurs because water molecules adhere to the tube walls and cohere to each other.
What does pH measure in a soil sample?
Which question is non-testable because it contains subjective terms?
options: How many leaves does the plant grow in one week?
Is loam soil the best soil for growing roses?
Does clay soil hold more water than sandy soil?
What is the height of the plant after ten days?

standard.q: What is the density of a 100-gram rock that displaces 20 milliliters of water?
True or False: Changing both the type of soil and the amount of water in a plant test is a confounding setup.
What do we call unexpected data points that do not align with the hypothesis?
Which of the following is an operational definition of plant growth rate?
options: The change in plant height in centimeters per day
The quality of the fruit produced

challenge.q: You are testing if temperature affects the viscosity of oil. You measure the flow time in seconds. What is the dependent variable?
How would you set up a control group to test if a new plant food accelerates tomato production?
options: Grow one plant with double fertilizer.
Grow a group of identical plants under the exact same conditions, but with NO plant food added.
Grow different plant species with the food.

challenge.q: A student measures the displacement of a toy figurine in a cylinder. The water rises from 50 mL to 68 mL. What is the volume of the figurine?
Which of the following is a testable hypothesis based on the question: 'Does surface texture affect top speed?'
options: If the surface is rough, the top will spin slower due to friction.
Rough surfaces are very bad for spinning tops.
Circular force keeps tops standing upright.
Spinning tops is a fun science game.

challenge.q: What error is committed if a scientist rejects data points that contradict their hypothesis without investigation?

performance.title: Experimental Variable & Protocol Architect
performance.desc: Identify a advanced inquiry question (e.g., 'How does salt concentration affect the surface tension of water measured by the number of drops on a coin?'). Formulate a testable hypothesis, write down a step-by-step experimental protocol including a control group, and construct a blank data table to record your variables.
labels: Wrote a mathematically testable hypothesis with isolated variables
Detailed a logical experimental protocol with a clear control setup
Designed a professional data table with labeled units (e.g., mL, drops, seconds)

pages:
Exercise 1: Testable vs. Non-Testable Questions

Classify each scientific query as either Testable (can be measured and tested) or Non-Testable (subjective or vague).

1. Is clay the most fun material to play with in class?
Non-Testable
2. Does heating modeling clay reduce the force needed to bend it by 50%?
Testable
3. Do plants like music better than quiet rooms?
Non-Testable

Exercise 2: Identifying Confounding Variables

Read the scenario below and identify the mistake in the experimental design.

Scenario: Matteo wants to test if wing size affects paper plane distance. He folds a small-winged plane using heavy cardboard and a large-winged plane using thin copy paper. He throws them both in the wind.

Identify the confounding variables that make this test unfair:
Matteo changed both the wing size and the paper thickness/weight,
making it impossible to know which factor affected the flight distance.

Exercise 3: Advanced Variable Control Protocol

Write down the operational definition and the baseline control for the following question.

Question: How does water salinity affect capillary action in cotton fabric?
1. Operational Definition of Capillary Action:
The height in millimeters that liquid climbs up a fabric strip in 5 minutes
2. Baseline Control Group:
A cotton fabric strip placed in pure tap water with zero salt added

slides.title: Welcome to the Animal Kingdom!
slides.text: Hello, Explorer! Welcome to English Week 2! This week, we are diving into the world of action. We will learn about words that start with double consonants. We will understand action verbs and how they show movement. We will ask questions like a scientist using question marks. We will also learn to read character feelings and traits. Let's keep our eyes open and watch animals in action. Are you ready to explore the animal kingdom today? For instance, consider the example of Weekly Goal: Master CCVC words, understand action verbs, ask question sentences, and infer feelings. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Weekly Goal
examples.content: Master CCVC words, understand action verbs, ask question sentences, and infer feelings.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Starting Sounds (CCVC)
slides.text: CCVC words stand for Consonant-Consonant-Vowel-Consonant. They start with a blend of two consonant sounds. Think of the word f-r-o-g. The 'f' and 'r' blend. We say them together quickly: frog! CCVC words are different from CVCC words. They have their consonant blends at the beginning. Let's practice saying beginning consonant blends today. They help us name many interesting things in nature! For instance, consider the example of Word Pattern: C = Consonant, C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Word Pattern
examples.content: C = Consonant, C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: The Green Frog
slides.text: In the wet pond, we observe a green frog. The word 'frog' starts with the 'fr' blend. We watch how it leaps from leaf to leaf. We spell the word as f - r - o - g. Listen to the beginning sound of the word 'frog'. The letters 'f' and 'r' join to make 'fr'. It is a wonderful CCVC word we see in ponds. Let's say 'frog' aloud and feel the blend! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: f - r - o - g

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: The Crawling Crab
slides.text: On the sandy beach, we spot a small crab. It crawls sideways along the wet sand. The word 'crab' starts with the 'cr' blend. We spell the word as c - r - a - b. How many legs does a crab use to crawl? Crabs are fascinating creatures in the shoreline habitat. Say 'crab' to hear the 'cr' starting sound. It is another CCVC word in our nature list! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
